qa_pairs.question: I just found out I'm pregnant. When should I go for my first antenatal visit?
qa_pairs.answer: Congratulations! You should go for your first antenatal visit as soon as possible, ideally before 16 weeks (4 months) of pregnancy. Early visits help detect problems early and ensure you and your baby stay healthy. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What tests will be done at my booking visit?
qa_pairs.answer: Standard pregnancy tests include: blood tests (anemia, HIV, syphilis, Hepatitis B, blood group, sickling), urine tests, blood pressure, and ultrasound scans. These are free under NHIS. Tests help detect problems early for timely treatment.

qa_pairs.question: How many antenatal visits should I attend during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: The Ghana Health Service recommends at least 4 ANC visits, but 8 visits is better for you and your baby. The schedule is: (1) First visit - as soon as you know you're pregnant, before 16 weeks. (2) Second visit - at 20-24 weeks (5-6 months). Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What are the danger signs during pregnancy that require immediate medical attention?
qa_pairs.answer: Seek immediate medical care for: heavy bleeding, severe headache with vision changes, convulsions/fits, severe abdominal pain, no fetal movement, water breaking before 37 weeks, high fever, or difficulty breathing. Call 112/193 for ambulance if needed. Don't delay - quick action saves lives.

qa_pairs.question: Can I work on my farm during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, you can continue light farming work during pregnancy - moderate exercise is good for you and your baby. However, make changes to protect yourself. What you CAN do: light weeding, planting seeds, harvesting vegetables, feeding animals (avoid animal waste contact). Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: I'm taking iron tablets but they make me very constipated. What can I do?
qa_pairs.answer: Take iron tablets as prescribed to prevent anemia. Take with vitamin C (orange juice) for better absorption, not with tea or milk. Constipation is a common side effect - drink plenty of water and eat high-fiber foods. Don't skip doses.

qa_pairs.question: What traditional Ghanaian foods are good for pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Eat a balanced diet including: proteins (fish, beans, eggs, meat), vegetables (kontomire, garden eggs, okra), fruits, whole grains, and dairy. Drink plenty of water. Avoid raw/undercooked foods, excessive salt, and alcohol. Traditional Ghanaian soups and stews are nutritious choices.

qa_pairs.question: How much water should I drink during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: You should drink at least 10-12 cups (8-ounce cups) of water daily during pregnancy - that's about 2.5-3 liters. Your body needs more fluids because: (1) Your blood volume increases by 50% during pregnancy. (2) Baby needs fluids for amniotic fluid. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: My eyes look very pale and I feel tired all the time. What's wrong?
qa_pairs.answer: You likely have anemia (low blood/insufficient red blood cells). Anemia is very common - 63 out of 100 pregnant women in Ghana have it. Signs: pale eyes (conjunctiva), pale tongue, pale palms/nails, feeling very tired and weak, dizziness (especially standing), difficulty breathing, fast heartbeat, headaches, sometimes craving clay or ice. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How can I prevent malaria during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Malaria is dangerous during pregnancy and can cause miscarriage, preterm birth, or low birth weight. Take your IPTp-SP preventive doses at ANC, sleep under treated mosquito nets, and seek treatment immediately if you have fever with chills. Prevention is key.

qa_pairs.question: I'm 5 months pregnant with severe headache and blurred vision. What should I do?
qa_pairs.answer: Go to the hospital IMMEDIATELY - this is an EMERGENCY. Severe headache with blurred vision can be pre-eclampsia, a very serious condition that can kill you and your baby. Other danger signs of pre-eclampsia: swelling of face and hands, upper stomach pain, seeing flashing lights/spots, severe dizziness. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What is the IPTp-SP medicine I receive at ANC and why is it important?
qa_pairs.answer: Always consult your healthcare provider before taking any medication during pregnancy. Some medicines are unsafe for your baby. Never self-medicate. Bring all medications to your ANC visit for review.

qa_pairs.question: I tested positive for HIV during my ANC visit. Can I still have a healthy baby?
qa_pairs.answer: With proper treatment, HIV-positive mothers can have healthy babies. Take your ARV medications as prescribed, attend all clinic appointments, and follow PMTCT guidelines. Your baby will be tested and may receive preventive treatment. Breastfeeding guidance depends on your situation.

qa_pairs.question: What foods should I avoid during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Eat a balanced diet including: proteins (fish, beans, eggs, meat), vegetables (kontomire, garden eggs, okra), fruits, whole grains, and dairy. Drink plenty of water. Avoid raw/undercooked foods, excessive salt, and alcohol. Traditional Ghanaian soups and stews are nutritious choices.

qa_pairs.question: What are the signs that labor is starting?
qa_pairs.answer: Signs labor is starting: regular contractions getting stronger and closer together, water breaking (clear fluid gush or trickle), bloody show (mucus with blood), and lower back pain. Go to the hospital when contractions are 5 minutes apart lasting 1 minute each, or immediately if water breaks.

qa_pairs.question: How will I know when to go to the hospital for delivery?

qa_pairs.question: What is cesarean section and when is it needed?

Discuss the reasons with your doctor. Recovery takes longer than vaginal birth but most women heal well.

qa_pairs.question: What is normal postpartum bleeding (lochia) and when should I be concerned?
qa_pairs.answer: Lochia is normal vaginal discharge/bleeding after delivery. Every woman has it as your womb heals. Normal pattern: (1) First 1-3 days (Lochia rubra): Heavy, bright red bleeding like heavy menstrual period. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How do I care for my C-section wound?
qa_pairs.answer: Proper C-section wound care prevents infection and helps healing. Your C-section wound has two parts: internal stitches (dissolve on their own) and external closure (stitches or staples, usually removed 5-7 days after surgery). DAILY CARE: (1) Keep wound clean and DRY. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: When should I start breastfeeding my baby?
qa_pairs.answer: Breastfeeding is best for your baby. Start within the first hour after birth. Feed on demand, usually 8-12 times daily. Ensure proper latch - baby's mouth should cover the areola, not just the nipple. Seek help from your midwife if you have difficulties.

qa_pairs.question: How do I know if my baby is latching properly for breastfeeding?
qa_pairs.answer: Breastfeeding is best for your baby. Start within the first hour after birth. Feed on demand, usually 8-12 times daily. Ensure proper latch - baby's mouth should cover the areola, not just the nipple. Seek help from your midwife if you have difficulties.

qa_pairs.question: Should I give my baby water in addition to breast milk?
qa_pairs.answer: NO! Your baby does NOT need water until 6 months old. Exclusive breastfeeding means ONLY breast milk - no water, no gripe water, no herbal tea, no formula, no glucose water, nothing else. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: My breasts are very full, hard, and painful. What should I do?
qa_pairs.answer: You have breast engorgement - very common around day 3-5 after delivery when milk 'comes in' in larger quantities. Breasts become overfull, hard, hot, shiny, painful. Baby may have difficulty latching onto hard breast. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How can I increase my breast milk supply?
qa_pairs.answer: Most women produce enough milk, but here's how to increase supply if truly low: (1) Breastfeed more frequently - milk production works on supply and demand. More baby sucks = more milk produced. Feed at least 8-12 times per 24 hours. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How do I care for my newborn's umbilical cord stump?
qa_pairs.answer: Proper cord care prevents serious infection (omphalitis) that can kill newborns. SAFE CORD CARE PRACTICE: (1) Keep cord DRY and CLEAN at all times. (2) Fold diaper below cord so it doesn't rub or get wet with urine. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: When should my baby receive immunizations?
qa_pairs.answer: Follow Ghana's Expanded Program on Immunization (EPI) schedule closely. Vaccines are FREE at all health facilities. AT BIRTH (within 24 hours): (1) BCG vaccine (against tuberculosis) - single dose in left upper arm. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What are the danger signs in a newborn that require immediate medical attention?
qa_pairs.answer: Seek immediate medical care for: heavy bleeding, severe headache with vision changes, convulsions/fits, severe abdominal pain, no fetal movement, water breaking before 37 weeks, high fever, or difficulty breathing. Call 112/193 for ambulance if needed. Don't delay - quick action saves lives.

qa_pairs.question: What is the difference between the pill, injection, and implant for family planning?
qa_pairs.answer: These are all hormonal contraceptives but differ in how they're used and duration: COMBINED ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL (COC): Contains estrogen + progesterone. Take 1 pill same time every day for 21 days, then 7-day break (or 7 inactive pills), then start new pack. Effectiveness: 91-99% with perfect use. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: My mother-in-law says I should not eat eggs during pregnancy because they will make delivery difficult. Is this true?

qa_pairs.question: Should I give my baby water or gripe water in the first days of life?
qa_pairs.answer: NO! Do not give water, gripe water, herbal drinks, or anything except breast milk for the first 6 months. TRADITIONAL PRACTICE: In many Ghanaian communities, it's customary to give newborns water, gripe water, glucose water, or herbal preparations in first days before breastfeeding or alongside breast milk. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: Is it safe to use traditional herbs during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Most foods are safe during pregnancy when properly prepared. Ensure it is fresh and well-cooked if applicable. If you have specific health conditions, consult your midwife about dietary restrictions.

qa_pairs.question: How is my pregnancy covered under NHIS? What services are free?
qa_pairs.answer: Great news! Since 2008, Ghana has FREE MATERNAL HEALTHCARE under the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS). Here's what's covered: REGISTRATION: Pregnant women get FREE NHIS registration - no premium payment, no three-month waiting period. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: Where should I deliver my baby - at home with TBA or at the health facility?
qa_pairs.answer: You should deliver at a HEALTH FACILITY with skilled birth attendants (doctor, midwife, or nurse). This is safest for you and your baby. Here's why: FACILITY DELIVERY BENEFITS: (1) Skilled attendants trained to manage normal delivery and complications. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What is a CHPS compound and what services do they provide?
qa_pairs.answer: CHPS stands for Community-Based Health Planning and Services. CHPS compounds are small health posts located in communities to bring basic healthcare closer to people, especially in rural areas. There are about 5,876 CHPS compounds across Ghana. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: I feel very sad after delivery, I don't want to take care of my baby, and I think about harming myself. What's wrong with me?

qa_pairs.question: Is it normal to feel anxious and worried during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Mild it can be normal during pregnancy due to hormonal and physical changes. However, if it's severe, persistent, or comes with warning signs like bleeding, fever, or severe pain, see your healthcare provider promptly.

qa_pairs.question: My 3-day-old baby's skin and eyes are yellow. Is this normal?
qa_pairs.answer: Mild this can be normal during pregnancy due to hormonal and physical changes. However, if it's severe, persistent, or comes with warning signs like bleeding, fever, or severe pain, see your healthcare provider promptly.

qa_pairs.question: How often should my newborn baby poop and pee?
qa_pairs.answer: Newborn stool and urine patterns are important indicators of adequate feeding and health. STOOLS (POOP): First 24-48 hours: Meconium - sticky, black/dark green, tar-like first stool. Baby should pass within 24 hours of birth. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: Should I bathe my newborn baby every day?
qa_pairs.answer: Bathing newborns requires special considerations. Here are evidence-based recommendations: FIRST BATH TIMING: Wait at least 24 hours after birth before first bath, preferably 48-72 hours. Here's why: (1) Vernix (white creamy coating on newborn skin) is protective - moisturizes skin, antibacterial properties, helps regulate temperature. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: Can I use shea butter or coconut oil on my baby's skin?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, pure shea butter and coconut oil are generally safe and beneficial for baby's skin after the neonatal period (first 28 days), with some precautions. SHEA BUTTER: Pure, unrefined shea butter is traditional in Ghana and has benefits: (1) Natural moisturizer for dry skin. (2) Anti-inflammatory properties. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What is the LAM method of family planning and does it really work?
qa_pairs.answer: LAM stands for Lactational Amenorrhea Method - it's natural family planning using exclusive breastfeeding to prevent pregnancy. When used correctly, LAM is 98-99% effective for first 6 months postpartum. HOW LAM WORKS: Exclusive, frequent breastfeeding suppresses ovulation (release of eggs) by keeping prolactin hormone high and suppressing ovulation hormones. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: I want permanent family planning. What are my options?
qa_pairs.answer: If you're certain you want no more children, permanent contraception (sterilization) is an option. These methods are PERMANENT and IRREVERSIBLE (extremely difficult and expensive to reverse), so choose only if you're absolutely sure. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How can I involve my husband in pregnancy care?
qa_pairs.answer: Partner involvement improves maternal and newborn outcomes significantly. Here are ways your husband can actively participate: DURING PREGNANCY: (1) ATTEND ANC VISITS: Encourage him to come to at least some visits, especially: booking visit, ultrasound scan, birth preparedness counseling. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: I'm 16 years old and pregnant. What special care do I need?
qa_pairs.answer: Teenage pregnancy (adolescent pregnancy, under 20 years) requires special attention because young mothers face higher risks. However, with good care, you and your baby can be healthy. Don't be ashamed - focus on getting the best care possible. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: When is the best time for my first ultrasound scan?
qa_pairs.answer: The optimal first ultrasound (dating scan) is between 8-14 weeks of pregnancy, ideally before 16 weeks at your booking visit. This early scan: (1) Confirms pregnancy location (intrauterine vs ectopic), (2) Establishes accurate gestational age (±5-7 days accuracy), (3) Determines expected delivery date, (4) Confirms fetal viability (heartbeat visible from 6 weeks), (5) Detects multiple pregnancy (twins/triplets), (6) Identifies some major abnormalities. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: I'm 8 months pregnant and my baby is in breech position. What does this mean?
qa_pairs.answer: Breech means baby's buttocks or feet are positioned to come out first instead of head-first (cephalic). Types: Frank breech (buttocks first, legs up), Complete breech (buttocks first, legs crossed), Footling breech (one or both feet first). Prevalence: 25% at 28 weeks, only 3-4% at term (most turn naturally). Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: Why do I need tetanus vaccination during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: This happens due to the hormonal and physical changes your body undergoes during pregnancy. It's usually normal, but discuss persistent or severe symptoms with your healthcare provider to rule out complications.

qa_pairs.question: What is the partograph and why is it used during labor?
qa_pairs.answer: The partograph is a graphical record of labor progress, maternal condition, and fetal wellbeing used by skilled birth attendants. It plots: (1) Cervical dilatation over time (should progress ~1cm/hour in active labor), (2) Fetal heart rate (normal 110-160 bpm), (3) Contractions (frequency, duration, strength), (4) Fetal descent, (5) Maternal vital signs (BP, pulse, temperature), (6) Urine output, (7) Medications given. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How can I prevent and treat heartburn during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: To prevent heartburn: eat small frequent meals, avoid spicy and fatty foods, don't eat close to bedtime, sleep propped up, avoid bending over after meals, and wear loose clothing. Antacids can help - check with your midwife.

qa_pairs.question: I have cravings for clay and sand during pregnancy. Is this dangerous?
qa_pairs.answer: Seek immediate medical care for: heavy bleeding, severe headache with vision changes, convulsions/fits, severe abdominal pain, no fetal movement, water breaking before 37 weeks, high fever, or difficulty breathing. Call 112/193 for ambulance if needed. Don't delay - quick action saves lives.

qa_pairs.question: Should I eat more food when pregnant?
qa_pairs.answer: Quality matters more than quantity. Energy needs increase only modestly: (1) First trimester: Same as pre-pregnancy (no extra calories needed). (2) Second trimester: Add 340 calories daily above baseline. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: What is gestational diabetes and how is it managed?
qa_pairs.answer: Gestational diabetes needs careful management to protect you and baby. Monitor blood sugar as advised, follow your meal plan, stay active, and take medication if prescribed. Well-controlled diabetes leads to healthy outcomes. Baby's size will be monitored.

qa_pairs.question: What is focused antenatal care?
qa_pairs.answer: Focused ANC (FANC) is goal-oriented approach adopted by WHO in 2016. Instead of many routine visits, focuses on quality over quantity with specific objectives each visit. Ghana follows WHO 2016 model: minimum 4 focused visits for healthy pregnancies, 8 contacts optimal. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: Can I attend ANC at a facility different from where I will deliver?
qa_pairs.answer: You CAN attend ANC at different facility from delivery, but STRONGLY NOT RECOMMENDED for safety. PROBLEMS: (1) Fragmented care - delivery facility lacks your complete history, test results, identified complications. (2) Emergency risks - unfamiliar team managing complications without knowing your risk factors. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.answer: Vaccinations during pregnancy protect you and your baby. Tetanus vaccine (TT) is given to prevent neonatal tetanus. Your baby will receive vaccines from birth including BCG, OPV, and others according to Ghana's immunization schedule. All vaccines are free at health facilities.

qa_pairs.question: Malaria prevention 0
qa_pairs.answer: Malaria is dangerous during pregnancy and can cause miscarriage, preterm birth, or low birth weight. Take your IPTp-SP preventive doses at ANC, sleep under treated mosquito nets, and seek treatment immediately if you have fever with chills. Prevention is key.
